chunk_summary: Guidance on investigating persistent growth faltering in children aged 6–24 months, focusing on feeding quality, red flags, and tailored diagnostic work-ups for underlying causes.
learning_objectives: Identify key red flags that warrant early investigation in cases of persistent growth faltering.
Understand the role of feeding quality, safety, and skill in addressing poor weight gain in children aged 6–24 months.
Learn the minimum outpatient work-up for persistent growth faltering, including specific tests and their indications.
Recognize region-specific considerations, such as chronic infections and malabsorption, in the differential diagnosis.
key_facts: Persistent growth faltering should be investigated if no weight gain occurs over 2–4 weeks despite addressing feeding issues.
Red flags include persistent vomiting, blood in stool, chronic diarrhea, recurrent infections, edema, and developmental regression.
Minimum outpatient work-up includes CBC, urine analysis/culture, stool evaluation, celiac screening (tissue transglutaminase IgA and total IgA), and TSH based on symptoms.
In India, consider tuberculosis, celiac disease, cow’s milk protein allergy, and hypothyroidism as potential causes.
Measurement quality (calibrated scale, correct length technique) must be confirmed before initiating investigations.
reference_quote: "If these are corrected and there is still no weight gain over 2–4 weeks, broaden the differential." (Source: NICE guideline NG75)
document_key_topics: Persistent growth faltering in children aged 6–24 months
Red flags and early investigation criteria
